title: Major Trauma — Primary Survey
oneLiner: Systematic ABCDE approach to identify and manage immediately life-threatening injuries in the first hour; every trauma patient.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Tension pneumothorax:** The finding that says "this is the killer" — absent breath sounds + hyperresonance + tracheal deviation away + shock. Needle decompression before any imaging.
- **Cardiac tamponade:** The finding that says "this is the killer" — Beck's triad (muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins, hypotension). Often missed in penetrating chest trauma. Bedside FAST is diagnostic.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Mechanism (blunt/penetrating, high/low energy). Altered consciousness if head injury. Dyspnoea, chest pain, abdominal pain, pelvic pain, limb deformity, visible haemorrhage.
- **Key exam findings:** Airway patency, respiratory rate/effort, chest symmetry, breath sounds, heart sounds, JVP, pulse character, capillary refill, skin temperature, GCS/AVPU, abdomen tenderness/distension, pelvic stability, long bone deformities, neurological deficit.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely:** Pattern based on mechanism (blunt: thoracic, abdominal, pelvic, long bone; penetrating: entry/exit wounds). In the shocked patient: haemorrhagic shock (flat neck veins, cool, narrow pulse pressure) vs. obstructive shock (distended neck veins, muffled sounds, equal breath sounds).
- **Splitter:** Bedside FAST — free fluid indicates haemorrhage; pericardial effusion indicates tamponade; no fluid + distended neck veins + unequal breath sounds → tension pneumothorax.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):** Continuous capnography (if intubated), pulse oximetry, BP, ECG, FAST scan. Do not delay life-saving interventions for imaging.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, coagulation, group & screen/crossmatch, lactate, ABG. Consider toxicology if altered GCS.
- **Imaging:** First-line: Chest X-ray (AP supine), Pelvis X-ray (if unstable). Second-line: CT head, C-spine, chest, abdomen/pelvis if haemodynamically stable. Whole-body CT in major trauma centres.
- **Special tests:** Diagnostic peritoneal aspiration (if FAST equivocal and unstable). E-FAST for pneumothorax.
- **Scoring:** Revised Trauma Score (RTS), Injury Severity Score (ISS). Massive transfusion trigger: ABC score or Shock Index (HR/SBP >1).
- **Never-miss test:** FAST scan in any haemodynamically unstable trauma patient. Also: manual pelvic compression (once) to assess stability – can reveal major retroperitoneal bleed.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** Activate major trauma team. C-spine immobilisation until cleared. Primary survey rigidly:
  - **A:** Maintain airway, jaw thrust, oropharyngeal/nasopharyngeal airway. Intubate if GCS <8 or airway compromised (RSI with cricoid pressure).
  - **B:** High-flow O2. Needle decompression if tension pneumothorax (2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line). Chest drain if haemothorax (5th intercostal space, midaxillary line). Seal open pneumothorax with three-sided dressing.
  - **C:** Control external haemorrhage (direct pressure, tourniquet, haemostatic dressings). Apply pelvic binder if suspected pelvic fracture. IV access x2 large bore (14G/16G). Start warmed crystalloid only if no blood available; target systolic BP ~90 mmHg in active bleeding (permissive hypotension). Call for massive transfusion protocol if shocked – give blood 1:1:1 (PRBC:FFP:platelets). Tranexamic acid 1g IV over 10 min within 3 hours.
  - **D:** AVPU/GCS, pupil size/reactivity, check blood glucose.
  - **E:** Expose completely, log roll to inspect back, maintain temperature (warm blankets, fluid warmer). Keep room warm.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Admit to HDU/ICU. Complete secondary survey (head-to-toe examination). Monitor for compartment syndrome (pain on passive stretch, tense limb, paraesthesia). Serial lactates and coagulation. Definitive surgical management as needed.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Rehabilitation, fracture clinic, psychology follow-up for PTSD.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** NBM until injury clearance. IV access (two cannulae). Maintenance fluids: Hartmann's solution 1-1.5L/24h if not bleeding. VTE prophylaxis: start LMWH after 24 hours if no ongoing bleeding and no coagulopathy. Observations: continuous monitoring initially, then NEWS2 4-hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Tranexamic acid:** 1g IV over 10 min within 3 hours of injury, then 1g infusion over 8 hours (CRASH-2). [Check local policy for timing/dose in paediatrics or if >3 hours]
- **Analgesia:** Morphine 2.5-5mg IV PRN (titrate to effect). Avoid IM if shocked.
- **Tetanus prophylaxis:** Human tetanus immunoglobulin 250 IU IM + vaccine (if wound risk and incomplete immunisation).
- **Antibiotics:** For open fractures or penetrating abdominal wounds: co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV 8-hourly. If penicillin allergic: cefuroxime 1.5g IV + metronidazole 500mg IV. [Check local policy]
- **Key contraindications:** Tranexamic acid after 3 hours increases harm. Avoid NSAIDs if at risk of bleeding or renal impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Morphine reduce dose in elderly/hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Underdosing tranexamic acid or giving it >3 hours post-injury. Also, giving excessive crystalloid leading to dilutional coagulopathy.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Haemorrhagic shock, coagulopathy, acidosis, hypothermia (lethal triad). Missed injury (e.g., tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade). Raised ICP. Acute kidney injury. Compartment syndrome. Infection (wound, chest, abdomen).
- **Chronic:** Chronic pain, PTSD, disability, non-union of fractures, post-thrombotic syndrome.
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality follows trimodal distribution: immediate (unsalvageable), early (hours – salvageable with good primary survey), late (days – due to sepsis/MOF). ISS >25 associated with >50% mortality.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always complete primary survey in order – fix each problem in A before moving to B. Do not be distracted by obvious limb injuries.
- Apply pelvic binder at the level of greater trochanters early – can prevent exsanguination from unstable pelvic fracture.
- Check blood glucose in all trauma patients with altered GCS – hypoglycaemia mimics head injury and is reversible.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- Do not remove C-spine immobilisation until cleared by clinical criteria (Canadian C-spine rule) or CT imaging.
- Do not give large volumes of crystalloid before haemorrhage control – permissive hypotension (SBP ~90 mmHg) until surgical bleeding control.
- Do not forget to log roll and examine the back – easily missed penetrating wounds or spinal injuries.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- Unlikely in major trauma – almost always admitted. For minor trauma:
  - **Return if:** Worsening pain, inability to weight bear, new neurological symptoms (numbness, weakness), fever, wound erythema/ discharge.
  - **Follow-up:** Fracture clinic or GP within 1-2 weeks; head injury advice sheet if concussion.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- Call trauma team leader / consultant if: airway compromise, respiratory rate <8 or >30, SpO2 <90% despite O2, systolic BP <90 mmHg, GCS <15 with deterioration, lactate >4 mmol/L, ongoing haemorrhage, NEWS2 ≥7.
- Call anaesthetist for difficult airway. Call ICU for organ support. Call paediatric trauma team if child (<16 years).

rawMarkdown:
## Major Trauma — Primary Survey
**One-liner:** Systematic ABCDE approach to identify and manage immediately life-threatening injuries in the first hour; every trauma patient.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Tension pneumothorax:** The finding that says "this is the killer" — absent breath sounds + hyperresonance + tracheal deviation away + shock. Needle decompression before any imaging.
- **Cardiac tamponade:** The finding that says "this is the killer" — Beck's triad (muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins, hypotension). Often missed in penetrating chest trauma. Bedside FAST is diagnostic.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Mechanism (blunt/penetrating, high/low energy). Altered consciousness if head injury. Dyspnoea, chest pain, abdominal pain, pelvic pain, limb deformity, visible haemorrhage.
- **Key exam findings:** Airway patency, respiratory rate/effort, chest symmetry, breath sounds, heart sounds, JVP, pulse character, capillary refill, skin temperature, GCS/AVPU, abdomen tenderness/distension, pelvic stability, long bone deformities, neurological deficit.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely:** Pattern based on mechanism (blunt: thoracic, abdominal, pelvic, long bone; penetrating: entry/exit wounds). In the shocked patient: haemorrhagic shock (flat neck veins, cool, narrow pulse pressure) vs. obstructive shock (distended neck veins, muffled sounds, equal breath sounds).
- **Splitter:** Bedside FAST — free fluid indicates haemorrhage; pericardial effusion indicates tamponade; no fluid + distended neck veins + unequal breath sounds → tension pneumothorax.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):** Continuous capnography (if intubated), pulse oximetry, BP, ECG, FAST scan. Do not delay life-saving interventions for imaging.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, coagulation, group & screen/crossmatch, lactate, ABG. Consider toxicology if altered GCS.
- **Imaging:** First-line: Chest X-ray (AP supine), Pelvis X-ray (if unstable). Second-line: CT head, C-spine, chest, abdomen/pelvis if haemodynamically stable. Whole-body CT in major trauma centres.
- **Special tests:** Diagnostic peritoneal aspiration (if FAST equivocal and unstable). E-FAST for pneumothorax.
- **Scoring:** Revised Trauma Score (RTS), Injury Severity Score (ISS). Massive transfusion trigger: ABC score or Shock Index (HR/SBP >1).
- **Never-miss test:** FAST scan in any haemodynamically unstable trauma patient. Also: manual pelvic compression (once) to assess stability – can reveal major retroperitoneal bleed.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** Activate major trauma team. C-spine immobilisation until cleared. Primary survey rigidly:
  - **A:** Maintain airway, jaw thrust, oropharyngeal/nasopharyngeal airway. Intubate if GCS <8 or airway compromised (RSI with cricoid pressure).
  - **B:** High-flow O2. Needle decompression if tension pneumothorax (2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line). Chest drain if haemothorax (5th intercostal space, midaxillary line). Seal open pneumothorax with three-sided dressing.
  - **C:** Control external haemorrhage (direct pressure, tourniquet, haemostatic dressings). Apply pelvic binder if suspected pelvic fracture. IV access x2 large bore (14G/16G). Start warmed crystalloid only if no blood available; target systolic BP ~90 mmHg in active bleeding (permissive hypotension). Call for massive transfusion protocol if shocked – give blood 1:1:1 (PRBC:FFP:platelets). Tranexamic acid 1g IV over 10 min within 3 hours.
  - **D:** AVPU/GCS, pupil size/reactivity, check blood glucose.
  - **E:** Expose completely, log roll to inspect back, maintain temperature (warm blankets, fluid warmer). Keep room warm.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Admit to HDU/ICU. Complete secondary survey (head-to-toe examination). Monitor for compartment syndrome (pain on passive stretch, tense limb, paraesthesia). Serial lactates and coagulation. Definitive surgical management as needed.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Rehabilitation, fracture clinic, psychology follow-up for PTSD.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** NBM until injury clearance. IV access (two cannulae). Maintenance fluids: Hartmann's solution 1-1.5L/24h if not bleeding. VTE prophylaxis: start LMWH after 24 hours if no ongoing bleeding and no coagulopathy. Observations: continuous monitoring initially, then NEWS2 4-hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Tranexamic acid:** 1g IV over 10 min within 3 hours of injury, then 1g infusion over 8 hours (CRASH-2). [Check local policy for timing/dose in paediatrics or if >3 hours]
- **Analgesia:** Morphine 2.5-5mg IV PRN (titrate to effect). Avoid IM if shocked.
- **Tetanus prophylaxis:** Human tetanus immunoglobulin 250 IU IM + vaccine (if wound risk and incomplete immunisation).
- **Antibiotics:** For open fractures or penetrating abdominal wounds: co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV 8-hourly. If penicillin allergic: cefuroxime 1.5g IV + metronidazole 500mg IV. [Check local policy]
- **Key contraindications:** Tranexamic acid after 3 hours increases harm. Avoid NSAIDs if at risk of bleeding or renal impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Morphine reduce dose in elderly/hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Underdosing tranexamic acid or giving it >3 hours post-injury. Also, giving excessive crystalloid leading to dilutional coagulopathy.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Haemorrhagic shock, coagulopathy, acidosis, hypothermia (lethal triad). Missed injury (e.g., tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade). Raised ICP. Acute kidney injury. Compartment syndrome. Infection (wound, chest, abdomen).
- **Chronic:** Chronic pain, PTSD, disability, non-union of fractures, post-thrombotic syndrome.

### Prognosis
- Mortality follows trimodal distribution: immediate (unsalvageable), early (hours – salvageable with good primary survey), late (days – due to sepsis/MOF). ISS >25 associated with >50% mortality.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always complete primary survey in order – fix each problem in A before moving to B. Do not be distracted by obvious limb injuries.
- Apply pelvic binder at the level of greater trochanters early – can prevent exsanguination from unstable pelvic fracture.
- Check blood glucose in all trauma patients with altered GCS – hypoglycaemia mimics head injury and is reversible.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- Do not remove C-spine immobilisation until cleared by clinical criteria (Canadian C-spine rule) or CT imaging.
- Do not give large volumes of crystalloid before haemorrhage control – permissive hypotension (SBP ~90 mmHg) until surgical bleeding control.
- Do not forget to log roll and examine the back – easily missed penetrating wounds or spinal injuries.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- Unlikely in major trauma – almost always admitted. For minor trauma:
  - **Return if:** Worsening pain, inability to weight bear, new neurological symptoms (numbness, weakness), fever, wound erythema/ discharge.
  - **Follow-up:** Fracture clinic or GP within 1-2 weeks; head injury advice sheet if concussion.

### Escalation Criteria
- Call trauma team leader / consultant if: airway compromise, respiratory rate <8 or >30, SpO2 <90% despite O2, systolic BP <90 mmHg, GCS <15 with deterioration, lactate >4 mmol/L, ongoing haemorrhage, NEWS2 ≥7.
- Call anaesthetist for difficult airway. Call ICU for organ support. Call paediatric trauma team if child (<16 years).
